content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Alexandra M. Barnhill 
Burke, Williams & Sorensen, LLP 
1901 Harrison Street, Suite 900 
Oakland, CA 94612 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-144 
Dear Ms. Barnhill: 
September 14, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict 
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as 
common law conflict of interest or Section l 090. Finally, the Commission does not act as a finder 
of fact when rendering advice. (/11 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
May Councilmember Howard participate in upcoming decisions pertaining to the following 
proposed projects: (I) Bradford Street Affordable Housing Project; (2) 612 Jefferson Avenue; (3) 
801 Brewster Street; (4) 851 Main Street; and (5) 1409 El Camino Real in light of her ownership 
interest in a hotel in the downtown area? 
CONCLUSION 
As discussed below, Councilmember Howard has a conflict of interest in the decisions 
related to these five proposed projects that prohibits her from participating in them. 
FACTS 
You stated that Redwood City is experiencing rapid growth in its downtown area, and 
several development projects are expected to come before the Planning Commission in the 
foreseeable future. Development in the downtown area is governed by the Downtown Precise Plan 
("DTPP"). The DTPP establishes land use, development, and urban design regulations for the 183-
acre DTPP area. It sets Maximum Allowable Development caps ("MAD Caps") of 2,500 net new 
dwelling units; 100,000 square feet of net new retail development; 500,000 square feet of net new 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections I 8110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Cahfomia Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory re_ferences are to Title 2, Di\'ision 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-144 
Page No. 2 
commercial office space; and 200 net new lodging rooms. For each project, any net new units 
and/or square footage are subtracted from the amount available for other projects under the relevant 
MAD Cap. 
As of May 11, 2016, after accounting for all approved projects, the following development· 
units are left: 
· 892 net new dwelling units; 
· 99,343 square feet of net new retail development; 
· 0 square feet of net new commercial office space; and 
· 200 net new lodging rooms remained available under the MAD Caps. 
Because no commercial office space remains available under the MAD Cap, no new office 
space may be developed unless (a) new square footage becomes available through removal of 
current office space elsewhere in the DTPP area or (b) the City Council increases the commercial 
office space MAD Cap through an amendment to the DTPP. You stated, however, that most of the 
projects expected to come before the Planning Commission involve some combination of 
residential and retail uses. There are enough remaining residential dwelling units and retail square 
feet of development under the relevant MAD Caps to allow for each of the proposed residential and 
retail projects. 
Together with her spouse, Councilmember Howard has a 4.34358% interest in Sequoia 
Main Street, LLC ("LLC"). The LLC owns the historic Sequoia Hotel at 800 Main Street, which is 
located within the City's DTPP area. The hotel consists of fifty-four rooms that have shared 
bathroom facilities. All rooms are rented on a weekly or monthly basis at below market rates.1 In 
addition to the hotel rooms, the building contains ground floor retail tenants who lease space from 
the LLC. The retail tenants currently include an antique store, a butcher shop, a liquor/grocery store, 
a nail salon, a book store and a tobacco shop. The hotel provides no automobile or bicycle parking. 
As minority owners in the LLC, Councilmember Howard and her husband receive a 
periodic distribution from the LLC derived from operating profits from the real estate and hotel 
operations which is comprised of business income, rental income and interest income. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
] By email dated September 7. 2016, Jonathan Smith. Deputy City Attorney for Redwood City. stated that after 
e:-ttensive inquiries, it was unclear whether typical renters at the Sequoia Hotel would qualify for low or very-low 
income affordable units that are planned for the Bradford Street, Habitat for Humanity, and Anton projects described 
below. Eligibility for those planned units, however, requires that an individual's income be both above and below 
certain thresholds. 


File No. A-16-t 44 
Page No. 3 
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. 
These interests are: 
I. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
investment3 worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 
87103(a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 
87103(b).) 
3. Any source of income,-1 except gifts or loans by a commercial lending 
institution made in the regular course of business on terms available to 
the public without regard to official status, aggregating five hundred 
dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, 
the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, 
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103(d).) 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or 
gifts aggregating [$460] or more in value provided to, received by, or 
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
6. Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental 
decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her 
immediate family. 
Councilmember Howard is a public official subject to the Act. Assuming her investment in 
the LLC is valued at $2,000 or more, she has an interest in the LLC as a business entity. (Section 
87103.) She also has an interest in the LLC as a source of income assuming she receives a 
distribution that equals or exceeds $500 in the 1 2  months prior to a governmental decision. (Section 
87103(c).)5 
3 "Indirect investment" is defined 10 include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section 
87103.) 
' "Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse and a pro rata 
share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse) o,vns directly, indirectly, 
or beneficially, a JO-percent or greater interest. (Section 82030(a).) 
s However, because she has less than IO-percent ownership interest in the LLC, the clients of the company are 
not sources of income to Councilmember Howard (Section 82030(a)), nor does she have an interest in the real property. 


File No. A-16-144 
Page No. 4 
Initially, it is important to note that a conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. 
And the foreseeability and materiality analysis must be conducted on a decision-by-decision basis, 
unless the decisions are inextricably interrelated.6 
Foreseeability 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Regulation 18701 provides that "[a] financial effect ... is presumed to be 
reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental 
decision before the official or the official's agency." None of Councilmember Howard's interests 
are explicitly involved in the decisions in question. Thus, pursuant to Regulation 18701 (b), if a 
financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, 
it is reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances 
not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. 
Materiality: Business Entity and Business Entity as Source of Income 
The standard to detenninc materiality for a business entity, and business entity as source of 
incornc,7 is set forth in Regulation I 8702.1 (b) (business entity - not explicitly involved). Materiality 
is found if "a prudent person with sufficient infonnation would find it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change ... in the value of a privately-held 
business entity." (Regulation 18702.1 (b ).) The regulation provides various examples of the types of 
decisions that may impact the analysis such as those that: 
Increase or decrease the amount of competition in the field in which the business 
entity is engaged; 
Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity 
supplies; 
Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment 
projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either 
temporarily or pennanently, the amount of business the business entity receives; 
[and] 
Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other 
project that would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws 
from the location of the project. (Regulation 18702. I (b)(2) - (5).) 
6 Decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when Che result of one decision will effectively de1ermine, affinn, 
nullify. or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation l 8706(b).) Based on your facts none of the decisions in 
question are interrelated. 
[ See Regulation 18702.3(a)(4). Where lhe source of income is a business entity, Regulation 18702.3 refers 
back to the standard of applied in 18702.1 for business entities. 


File No. A-16-144 
Page No. 5 
We now apply these principles to the specific projects proposed in your letter. 
Bradford Street Affordable Housing Project 
This project proposes to construct a six-story, mixed-use use building at 707 and 777 
Bradford Street. The site includes four existing parcels, including two parcels that were acquired in 
2004 and 2006 with former Redwood City Redevelopment Agency Low-Moderate Housing Set 
Aside funds. The project would include approximately 108 units of rental housing affordable for 
very low income seniors, space for childcare or a nonprofit, and a publicly accessible park. The 
project is approximately 860 feet from the Sequoia Hotel. 
612 Jefferson Avenue ("Habitat for Humanity") 
Habitat for Humanity, Greater San Francisco, has applied for a Planned Community Permit 
to construct a six-story, 25,000 square foot residential building at 612 Jefferson Avenue. The 
project would consist of approximately 20 affordable housing units, and Habitat for Humanity will 
submit a related affordable housing plan for approval to the Planning Commission. The project may 
not meet the DTPP parking requirements and may require an allowance for such under provision of 
on-site parking. This project site is 805 feet away from the Sequoia Hotel. 
801 Brewster Street ("Anton") 
Anton Development Company proposes to construct a residential building at 801 Brewster 
Avenue. The Project would consist of approximately 50 very low income affordable multi-family 
units and 200 market-rate units. In connection with the project, the Planning Commission would be 
asked to approve a Planned Community Permit and an affordable housing plan. The site is 1,965 
away from the Sequoia Hotel. 
We consider these three decisions in light of our recent letter that analyzed the same five 
proposed projects in relation to another official's interests. In the Smii/1 Advice Letter, No. A-16-
102, the official had an interest in a company { Golden Fox Venues {"GFV")) which owns and 
operates multiple entertainment spaces in downtown Redwood City. There, with respect to these 
three decisions, we stated: 
While the decisions will add population to the general area, all 
are far enough away as to not substantially affect GFV's business or 
property. Any improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as 
redevelopment projects, traffic/road improvements, or parking changes 
that may affect, either temporarily or permanently, would not appear to 
affect GFV. 
Moreover, some of the projects will appear to have minimal 
effect on the business and clientele of GFV's businesses. You noted that 
the vast majority of sales at the GFV venues in Redwood City come 
through advance, online ticket sales and reservations rather than from 
walk-up business. The projects would not appear to impact marketability 
of GFV's business. These projects are small residential developments. 


File No. A-1 6-144 
Page No. 6 
For example, the closest is the Habitat for Humanity project to construct 
a six-story, 25,000 square foot residential building with 20 affordable 
housing units. This project will appear to have minimal impact on the 
area where GFV's properties are located. 
This would also appear to be the case with respect to the MidPen 
project [Bradford Street] which is 1,270 feet away from the Fox Theater 
parcel and 1 ,754 feet from the Fox Forum, and the Anton project located 
1,509 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 1 ,330 feet from the Fox 
Forum. The MidPen project involves the construction of 108 units of 
rental housing affordable for very low income seniors. The Anton 
project would consist of approximately 50 very low income affordable 
multi-family units and 200 market-rate units. Thus, none of the projects 
will likely have any foreseeable financial effect on the value of GFV and 
the Commissioner may participate in the MidPen, Anton and Habitat for 
Humanity decisions. 
There are important factual differences between the Smith Advice Letter, supra, and the 
present situation. For example, unlike the vast majority of sales at the GFV venues that come 
through admnce, 011/inc ticket sales and reservations, the Sequoia Hotel has several retail shops 
such as the liquor/grocery store, nail salon and tobacco shop that undoubtedly rely on walk-up 
business. By adding population to the general area, each of these projects potentially adds to any 
existing customer base already enjoyed by these shops. And it is safe to assume that such a potential 
increase in retail traffic would certainly increase the value of the Hotel's retail leases. In addition, at 
least two of the projects are in much closer proximity to the Sequoia Hotel (both within 900 feet) 
than the GFV venues. 
Moreover, although it could not be determined whether typical renters of the Hotel could 
qualify for the new low to very low income housing units, the possibility exists that such low 
income housing units could pose competition to the Sequoia Hotel rooms that are typically rented 
out on a weekly or monthly basis. 
Taking these differences into account, and looking to the applicable examples of decisions 
that might impact the value of a privately-held business under Regulation 1 8702. l (b ), we believe a 
prudent person could find it reasonably foreseeable that all three proposed projects will contribute 
to a change in the value of the Councilmember Howard's business interest. Thus, she may not 
participate in the decisions.8 
' When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a city councilmember) has a 
conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must; ( 1 )  immediately prior to the discussion of 
the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well aS details of the economic 
interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the 
discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105.) 


851 Main Street 
File No. A-16-144 
Page No. 7 
The Acclaim Companies has applied for a Planned Community Permit and a Tentative 
Parcel Map to construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building consisting of 
approximately 86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square feet of retail and 
80,000 square feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project, which would combine several existing 
lots, proposes to include two levels of underground parking with approximately 215 parking stalls 
where 260 are required by the DTPP and proposes to pay a parking in-lieu fee for the 45 spaces not 
provided on site. Acclaim also proposes to provide 56 bicycle spaces. 
Because the project would exceed the office MAD Cap under the DTPP, Acclaim is seeking 
an amendment to the General Plan and DTPP to allow for more office development. In addition, 
because the project would exceed the DTPP MAD Cap contemplated in the DTPP EIR, a new EIR 
will be prepared. The Acclaim site is 185 feet away from the Sequoia Hotel. 
1409 El Camino Real 
Greystar Development has applied for a Planned Community Pennit and Tentative Parcel 
Map to construct an eight-story, approximately 346-unit multifamily residential development with 
approximately 6,000 square feet of ground floor retail and two levels of under1:,1Tound parking at 
1409 El Camino Real. Of the 346 units, 36 will be affordable housing units on-site, and Greystar 
will submit a related affordable housing plan for approval to the Planning Commission. Greystar 
proposes to provide approximately 403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile spaces 
for retail use, and 93 bicycle spaces. The 1409 El Camino Real project site is 1,640 feet away from 
the Sequoia Hotel. 
Again, we find it helpful to consider the analysis already provided in the Smith Advice 
Letter, supra, to detennine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that these two proposed projects 
will have a material financial effect on the Sequoia Hotel: 
Regulation 18702.l(b)(5) provides an example of materiality where the 
decision is the location of a major development that would increase or 
decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the 
project. The Greystar site is 1,764 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 
2,151 from the Fox Forum. They applied to construct an eight-story, 
approximately 
346-unit 
multifamily 
residential 
development 
with 
approximately 6,000 feet of ground floor retail and two levels of underground 
parking at 1409 El Camino Real. Greystar proposes to provide approximately 
403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile spaces for retail use, 
and 93 bicycle spaces. The Acclaim site is l, l 40 feet away from the Fox 
Theater parcel and 1,9 l 5 feet from the Fox Forum. Acclaim intends to 
construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building consisting of 
approximately 86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square 
feet of retail and 80,000 square feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project 
will include two levels of underground parking with approximately 215 
parking stalls. 


File No. A-1 6-144 
Page No. 8 
These are major developments in proximity to GFV's properties that could 
increase the desirability of renting GFV facilities or attending GFV events. A 
prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in 
the value of GFV. Therefore, we conclude that there will be a foreseeable and 
material financial effect resulting from these project decisions and the 
Commissioner will have a conflict of interest and may not participate. 
In the present matter, the Sequoia Hotel is closer in proximity than any of the GFV venues 
to both of the proposed projects. In fact, it is just 1 85 feet from the Acclaim site. And similar to the 
situation in Smith, these are major developments in close proximity to the Sequoia Hotel that could 
increase the amount of business done by the retail entities thereby increasing the desirability of 
leasing its retail space. Based on these facts, we believe a prudent person would find it reasonably 
foreseeable that both proposed projects will contribute to a change in the value of Councilmcmber 
Howard's business interest. Thus, she may not participate in these two decisions. 
Accordingly, we conclude that Councilmember Howard has a prohibitory conflict of interest 
based on our determination that there will be a foreseeable and material financial effect resulting 
from all five decisions on the proposed projects. 
JW:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (9 16) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel

sections.question:
May Councilmember Howard participate in upcoming decisions pertaining to the following 
proposed projects: (I) Bradford Street Affordable Housing Project; (2) 612 Jefferson Avenue; (3) 
801 Brewster Street; (4) 851 Main Street; and (5) 1409 El Camino Real in light of her ownership 
interest in a hotel in the downtown area?
sections.conclusion:
As discussed below, Councilmember Howard has a conflict of interest in the decisions 
related to these five proposed projects that prohibits her from participating in them.

commercial office space; and 200 net new lodging rooms. For each project, any net new units 
and/or square footage are subtracted from the amount available for other projects under the relevant

As of May 11, 2016, after accounting for all approved projects, the following development· 
units are left: 
· 892 net new dwelling units; 
· 99,343 square feet of net new retail development;

· 200 net new lodging rooms remained available under the MAD Caps.

Forum. The MidPen project involves the construction of 108 units of

the Commissioner may participate in the MidPen, Anton and Habitat for 
Humanity decisions. 
There are important factual differences between the Smith Advice Letter, supra, and the 
present situation. For example, unlike the vast majority of sales at the GFV venues that come 
through admnce, 011/inc ticket sales and reservations, the Sequoia Hotel has several retail shops 
such as the liquor/grocery store, nail salon and tobacco shop that undoubtedly rely on walk-up 
business. By adding population to the general area, each of these projects potentially adds to any 
existing customer base already enjoyed by these shops. And it is safe to assume that such a potential 
increase in retail traffic would certainly increase the value of the Hotel's retail leases. In addition, at 
least two of the projects are in much closer proximity to the Sequoia Hotel (both within 900 feet) 
than the GFV venues. 
Moreover, although it could not be determined whether typical renters of the Hotel could 
qualify for the new low to very low income housing units, the possibility exists that such low 
income housing units could pose competition to the Sequoia Hotel rooms that are typically rented 
out on a weekly or monthly basis. 
Taking these differences into account, and looking to the applicable examples of decisions 
that might impact the value of a privately-held business under Regulation 1 8702. l (b ), we believe a 
prudent person could find it reasonably foreseeable that all three proposed projects will contribute 
to a change in the value of the Councilmember Howard's business interest. Thus, she may not 
participate in the decisions.8 
' When a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a city councilmember) has a 
conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must; ( 1 )  immediately prior to the discussion of 
the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well aS details of the economic 
interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the 
discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105.) 


851 Main Street  
The Acclaim Companies has applied for a Planned Community Permit and a Tentative 
Parcel Map to construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building consisting of 
approximately 86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square feet of retail and 
80,000 square feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project, which would combine several existing 
lots, proposes to include two levels of underground parking with approximately 215 parking stalls 
where 260 are required by the DTPP and proposes to pay a parking in-lieu fee for the 45 spaces not 
provided on site. Acclaim also proposes to provide 56 bicycle spaces. 
Because the project would exceed the office MAD Cap under the DTPP, Acclaim is seeking 
an amendment to the General Plan and DTPP to allow for more office development. In addition, 
because the project would exceed the DTPP MAD Cap contemplated in the DTPP EIR, a new EIR 
will be prepared. The Acclaim site is 185 feet away from the Sequoia Hotel. 
1409 El Camino Real 
Greystar Development has applied for a Planned Community Pennit and Tentative Parcel 
Map to construct an eight-story, approximately 346-unit multifamily residential development with 
approximately 6,000 square feet of ground floor retail and two levels of under1:,1Tound parking at 
1409 El Camino Real. Of the 346 units, 36 will be affordable housing units on-site, and Greystar 
will submit a related affordable housing plan for approval to the Planning Commission. Greystar 
proposes to provide approximately 403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile spaces 
for retail use, and 93 bicycle spaces. The 1409 El Camino Real project site is 1,640 feet away from 
the Sequoia Hotel. 
Again, we find it helpful to consider the analysis already provided in the Smith Advice 
Letter, supra, to detennine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that these two proposed projects 
will have a material financial effect on the Sequoia Hotel: 
Regulation 18702.l(b)(5) provides an example of materiality where the 
decision is the location of a major development that would increase or 
decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the 
project. The Greystar site is 1,764 feet away from the Fox Theater parcel and 
2,151 from the Fox Forum. They applied to construct an eight-story, 
approximately 
346-unit 
multifamily 
residential 
development 
with 
approximately 6,000 feet of ground floor retail and two levels of underground 
parking at 1409 El Camino Real. Greystar proposes to provide approximately 
403 automobile spaces for residential use, 36 automobile spaces for retail use, 
and 93 bicycle spaces. The Acclaim site is l, l 40 feet away from the Fox 
Theater parcel and 1,9 l 5 feet from the Fox Forum. Acclaim intends to 
construct a four-story mixed-use office and retail building consisting of 
approximately 86,910 gross square feet, including approximately 6,900 square 
feet of retail and 80,000 square feet of office at 851 Main Street. The project 
will include two levels of underground parking with approximately 215 
parking stalls. 


File No. A-1 6-144  
These are major developments in proximity to GFV's properties that could 
increase the desirability of renting GFV facilities or attending GFV events. A 
prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in 
the value of GFV. Therefore, we conclude that there will be a foreseeable and 
material financial effect resulting from these project decisions and the 
Commissioner will have a conflict of interest and may not participate. 
In the present matter, the Sequoia Hotel is closer in proximity than any of the GFV venues 
to both of the proposed projects. In fact, it is just 1 85 feet from the Acclaim site. And similar to the 
situation in Smith, these are major developments in close proximity to the Sequoia Hotel that could 
increase the amount of business done by the retail entities thereby increasing the desirability of 
leasing its retail space. Based on these facts, we believe a prudent person would find it reasonably 
foreseeable that both proposed projects will contribute to a change in the value of Councilmcmber 
Howard's business interest. Thus, she may not participate in these two decisions. 
Accordingly, we conclude that Councilmember Howard has a prohibitory conflict of interest 
based on our determination that there will be a foreseeable and material financial effect resulting 
from all five decisions on the proposed projects. 
JW:jgl

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Howard participate in upcoming decisions pertaining to the following 
proposed projects: (I) Bradford Street Affordable Housing Project; (2) 612 Jefferson Avenue; (3) 
801 Brewster Street; (4) 851 Main Street; and (5) 1409 El Camino Real in light of her ownership 
interest in a hotel in the downtown area?

CONCLUSION: As discussed below, Councilmember Howard has a conflict of interest in the decisions 
related to these five proposed projects that prohibits her from participating in them.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION • 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Alexandra M. Barnhill Burke, Williams & Sorensen, LLP 1901 Harrison Street, Suite 900 Oakland, CA 94612 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-144 Dear Ms. Barnhill: September 14, 2016 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section l 090. Finally, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. (/11 re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTION May Councilmember Howard participate in upcoming decisions pertaining to the following proposed projects: (I) Bradford Street Affordable Housing Project; (2) 612 Jefferson Avenue; (3) 801 Brewster Street; (4) 851 Main Street; and (5) 1409 El Camino Real in light of her ownership interest in a hotel in the downtown area? CONCLUSION As discussed below, Councilmember Howard has a conflict of interest in the decisions related to these five proposed projects that prohibits her from participating in them. FACTS You stated that Redwood City is experiencing rapid growth in its downtown area, and several development projects are expected to come before the Planning Commission in the foreseeable future. Development in the downtown area is governed by the Downtown Precise Plan ("DTPP"). The DTPP establishes land use, development, and urban design regulations for the 183- acre DTPP area. It sets Maximum Allowable Development caps ("MAD Caps") of 2,500 net new dwelling units; 100,000 square feet of net new retail development; 500,000 square feet of net new 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Cahfomia Code of Regulations. All regulatory re_ferences are to Title 2, Di\'ision 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-144 Page No. 2 commercial office space; and 200 net new lodging rooms. For each project, any net new units and/or square footage are subtracted from the amount available for other projects under the relevant MAD Cap. As of May 11, 2016, after accounting for all approved projects, the following development· units are left: · 892 net new dwelling units; · 99,343 square feet of net new retail development; · 0 square feet of net new commercial office space; and · 200 net new lodging rooms remained available under the MAD Caps. Because no commercial office space remains available under the MAD Cap, no new office space may be developed unless (a) new square footage becomes available through removal of current office space elsewhere in the DTPP area or (b) the
